revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/88383661|倭国造]]"
MaitreyaVaruna moved page [[Yamato Kokuzo]] to [[Draft:Yamato Kokuzo]]: botched translation, harder to translate than it looked

Disable the categories on this page while it is still a draft, per [[WP:DRAFTNOCAT]]/[[WP:USERNOCAT]] (using [[User:DannyS712/Draft no cat|Draft no cat]] v1.8). The easiest way to do this is by converting them to links, by adding a colon: "[[Category:" → "[[:Category:"
/* See Also */ took from [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)]] probably not useful but actually discusses this
Immanuelle moved page [[Draft:Yamato Kokuzo]] to [[Draft:Yamato Kuni-no-miyatsuko]]: actually linked title

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{AfC comment|This https://www.japanese-wiki-corpus.org/history/Yamato%20no%20Kuni%20no%20Miyatsuko.html seems to be a translation of this. This is only english language source I could find on this https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868 prognisis very bad for future development of this draft [[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 20:21, 17 March 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese clan}} ''''Yamato Kokuzo'''' was a [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] who ruled the central region of the later [[Yamato Province]]. Their [[Ujigami]] is {{Ill|Yamato Okunitama|lt=Yamato Okunitama|ja|倭大国魂神}} of [[Ōyamato Shrine]] __TOC__ {{Clear}} == Overview == === Names === In the Kiki they are called {{Nihongo|2=倭国造}} and in the [[Kujiki]] they are called {{Nihongo|2=大倭国造}} === ancestor === * In the Kojiki, after [[Emperor Jimmu|Wakamoge-no-Mikoto]] left Takashima Palace in [[Kibi Province|Kibi Province]], he went to Hayasukumon {{Efn|thought to be the Toyo Straits, Akashi Straits, Kibi, etc.}}. and appointed '''Konezu-no-hiko''', a Kunitsugami deity who had guided him and made offerings to him, to the position of Yamakazo. * In the Nihon-shoki (Chronicles of Japan), Shiinetsuhiko (Chinhiko), whom he met soon after leaving Hyuga, was appointed for the first time as the creator of Yamato in the 2nd year of Emperor Jinmu's reign, in recognition of his success in the Jinmu Eastern Expedition. * The "Nihon-shoki" (Chronicles of Japan) "Kokuzo honki" tells that Shiinetsuhiko-no-mikoto, a grandson of [[Hoori|Hiko Hidemi no Mikoto]], who met and piloted the boat in Oyamakuni, was later appointed as the founder of Oyamakuni. * In genealogical documents, Shiinetsuhiko is also called '''Uzubiko''', and in the "Yamato Shukune and Uminao" genealogy in the "Hyakke Genealogical Manuscripts" and the "Moro Genealogical Records", he is regarded as the grandson of Nurutama no Mikoto, the son of '''[[Watatsumi|Watatsumi Toyotamahiko-no-mikoto]]'''. === Clan === The name of the clan changed as the name of the country changed (Oyamatoji, Oyotokuji, Yamatoji, etc.). In [[Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]]10 ([[681|681]]), Yamato Ryūmarō was given the [[Muraji|Ren]] surname, in [[12]] the family became Ren, in [[14]] the surname became Imazun, and in [[Nara period|Nara era]] Yamato Nagaoka and Mizumori were promoted to [[Sukune|Sukune]]. Their distant ancestor, Shiinetsuhiko, is said to be the great-grandson of [[Watatsumi|Watatsumi Toyotamahiko-no-mikoto]], and the family is classified as a Jigion by deity. He is also said to be of the same clan as the Yagi clan and the [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]]. Descendants include the Kubiki Kunizo, Aomi, Akashi Kunizo, and Kibi-Kaibe clans. Note that the Wa are descended from [[Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong]], king of [[Baekje|Baekje]], so they are a separate lineage. == base == Is it Cape Anaso-eup Oyachooka (former shrine site of Yamato Shrine)? === ruling domain === The area ruled by the Kunizo was then called '''Yamato Province''', later ''''[[Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]''', which is now [[Nara Prefecture]] [[Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]] and the surrounding area. (eastern part of the Nara Basin) was also ruled. == Ujigami == * ''''[[Ōyamato Shrine|Yamato Za Okunitama Shrine]]''' (Ōyamato Shrine) *: Or, Yamato Okunitama Shrine. In the 7th year of Emperor Sujin's reign ([[91 BC|91 BC]]), Princess Nu-no-shiroiru, who was the chief priestess of the shrine, lost her hair and became too thin to continue her rituals, and in a dream, she was told by the oracle [[Emperor]] that "If you make Iso-nagao City the host of a shrine to the deity Yamato Okunitama, the kingdom will be in peace. Suinin|Taruhito]] dynasty, Ichiso-Nagao City was founded as a shrine to the deity of the Great Goddess of the Sea of Japan. === Tomb === * ''''Nakayama Otsuka Tumulus''' *: A front-recessed circular mound constructed in the early Kofun period, with Mikoto Sukune assumed to be its burial deity. * '''Hatagozuka burial mound'''. *: A forward-backward burial mound constructed in the early Kofun period, with Ichiso-Nagao City as the supposed interred object. * '''Shimoikeyama Tumulus'''. *: A forward-backward mound constructed in the early Kofun period, with Gojuno Sukune as the supposed interred person. == Person == * '''Mimonosukune'''. *: [[Kofun period|Kofun dynasty]] figure and national governor of the [[Emperor Sujin|Sungjin dynasty]]. Fifth grandson of Shiinetsuhiko. * ''''Ichishino-Nagaoichi'''' *: A Kofun period figure and national governor of the [[Emperor Suinin|Taruhin dynasty]]. Son of [[Mikazukune]]. * ''''Isono Sukune''''. *: A figure of the Kofun period, son of Ichiso-Nagaoichi. * '''Kate-no-Sukune'''. *: A figure of the Kofun period, son of Iguno Sukune. * '''Naruko Sukune'''. *: A personage of the Kofun period, son of Mukote Sukune. * '''Yamato Agoko Kagome'''. *: Kofun-era figure and national governor from the [[Emperor Nintoku|Nintoku dynasty]] to the [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku dynasty]]. Son of Naruko Shukune and brother of Somaro. He was almost killed by [[Emperor Richū|Renchū]] in a dispute over the succession to the throne, but he was saved by offering his sister Hinohime as an uname. Since then, it has become a custom for the Yamato clan to offer Une no Ehime to the Great King's family. * ''''Somaro'''' *: A figure of the Kofun period and brother of Agokago. Also known as Maro Shukune, he was given the surname Oyamachinao. * '''Nakuino-Atai'''. *: A figure of the Kofun period, son of Soumaro. * '''Yukiniwa no Atai''''. *: A personage of the Kofun period, son of Namaikinao. * '''Tehiko Atai'''. *: Kofun period general and [[Emperor Kinmei|Kinmei Dynasty]] kokuzo. Son of Yukiniwa Nao, he was active in the [[Silla|Silla]] wars against [[Silla]]. == descendants == * Oyamato Gohyakusoku *: There is a genealogical record that he was a son of Oyamato Ryumaro (Suzuki Shinnen, Hyakke Genealogical Manuscripts, vol. 21). Oyamakuninuzo, Ujigami, Gyobu Shosuke. *: Yamato Nagaoka *: [[Nara period|Nara era]] jurist. Son of Iotari (io-tari) of Oyamato. He was involved in the compilation of the [[Yōrō Code|Yōrō Ritsudō]]. His first name was Oyamato Imosun Kotohito. He later became Oyotoku Sukune, then Oyotoku Sukune, then Oyotoku Sukune again, and finally Yamato Sukune Nagaoka. He was also a Gyobu Shosuke, Minbu Daisuke and Kunmiya Daitada, Settsu Ryo, Mikawa Mamoru, Kawachi Mamoru, deputy chief of the Saikai Province patrol, and Sakyo Daio. Yamato-kunizo, Sho shikko-ranked. Compilation of the Reform Ritsuryo. * Yamato Tateko. *: A palm retainer of the Nimyo dynasty. He was given the surname of Asaomi. Later became a courtier and a fourth-ranked attendant. == Family tree == {{familytree/start}} {{familytree|001|border=0|001=[[Amaterasu|{{Bgcolor|#F2CEE0|Amaterasu}}]]}} {{familytree||!||border=0}} {{familytree|001|border=0|001=[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]}} {{familytree||!||border=0}} {{familytree|001|11=003|002=[[Konohanasakuya-hime|{{Bgcolor|#F2CEE0|Konohanasakuya-hime}}]]|001=[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]|border=0|14=|13=|12=|10=|~|9=|8=|7=|6=|5=002|4=~|y|003=[[Watatsumi]]）}} {{familytree||13=v|21=|20=|19=.|18=-|17=-|16=+|15=-|14=-|12=-||11=,|10=|9=|8=|7=|6=|5=!|4=||border=0}} {{familytree||10=|003={{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命}}|002=[[Toyotama-hime|{{Bgcolor|#F2CEE0|Toyotama-hime}}]]|001=[[Hoori]]|border=0|12=004|11=003|9=!||8=002|7=~|6=y|5=~|4=001||004={{ill|Nurutama-no-mikoto|ja|布留多摩命}}}} {{familytree||13=!|21=|20=|19=!|18=|17=|16=!|15=|14=|12=||11=|10=|9=|8=!|7=|6=|5=|4=||border=0}} {{familytree||10=002|003=（[[Azumi people]]）|002='''{{Bgcolor|#F2CEE0|Tamayori-hime}}'''|001='''[[Ugayafukiaezu]]'''|border=0|12=004|11=003|9=~||8=y|7=001|6=|5=|4=||004=（{{Interlanguage link|Owari Kuni-no-miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造}}, {{Interlanguage link|Yamato Kuni-no-miyatsuko|ja|倭国造}})}} {{familytree||13=-|21=|20=|19=|18=|17=|16=|15=|14=.|12=-||11=v|10=^|9=-|8=v|7=-|6=-|5=,|4=||border=0}} {{familytree||10=|003={{ill|Mikeiri no Mikoto|ja|御毛沼命}}|002={{ill|Inahi|ja|稲飯命}}|001={{ill|Itsuse|ja|五瀬命}}|border=0|12=|11=|9=||8=|7=004|6=003|5=002|4=001||004=[[Jimmu]]}} {{familytree||13=|21=|20=|19=|18=|17=|16=|15=|14=!|12=||11=|10=|9=|8=|7=|6=|5=|4=||border=0}} {{familytree||12=|border=0|18=|17=|16=|15=|14=|13=001|11=||10=|9=|8=|7=|6=|5=|4=||001=（[[Imperial House of Japan]]）}} {{familytree/end}}
* {{Bgcolor|#F2CEE0|赤背景}}は女性。
出典：{{Sfn|古代豪族系図集覧|p=7}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Daisho Tamashii Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[Kono Shrine]] * {{ill|Yamatotohimomo Hiyakusatsu Himemei|ja|倭迹迹日百襲媛命}} == 出典 == * 武光誠『古事記・日本書紀を知る事典』東京堂出版、[[1999|1999年]][[September|9月]]。ISBN 4-490-10526-6 == 参考文献 == * 『國史大辭典』 吉川弘文館 * 坂本太郎・平野邦雄 『日本古代氏族人名辞典』 吉川弘文館 * 『日本史広辞典』 山川出版社 * 『神道大辞典』 臨川書店 == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Draft Categories| [[Category:Japanese history stubs]] [[Category:History of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Province]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] }} {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=March 2022}}

This is only english language source I could find on this https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868 prognisis very bad for future development of this draft [[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 20:21, 17 March 2023 (UTC)}}
{{Infobox Japanese clan}}
''''Yamato Kokuzo'''' was a [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] who ruled the central region of the later [[Yamato Province]].
Their [[Ujigami]] is {{Ill|Yamato Okunitama|lt=Yamato Okunitama|ja|倭大国魂神}} of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]
__TOC__ {{Clear}}
== Overview ==
=== Names === In the Kiki they are called {{Nihongo|2=倭国造}} and in the [[Kujiki]] they are called {{Nihongo|2=大倭国造}}
=== ancestor ===
* In the Kojiki, after [[Emperor Jimmu|Wakamoge-no-Mikoto]] left Takashima Palace in [[Kibi Province|Kibi Province]], he went to Hayasukumon {{Efn|thought to be the Toyo Straits, Akashi Straits, Kibi, etc.}}. and appointed '''Konezu-no-hiko''', a Kunitsugami deity who had guided him and made offerings to him, to the position of Yamakazo. * In the Nihon-shoki (Chronicles of Japan), Shiinetsuhiko (Chinhiko), whom he met soon after leaving Hyuga, was appointed for the first time as the creator of Yamato in the 2nd year of Emperor Jinmu's reign, in recognition of his success in the Jinmu Eastern Expedition. * The "Nihon-shoki" (Chronicles of Japan) "Kokuzo honki" tells that Shiinetsuhiko-no-mikoto, a grandson of [[Hoori|Hiko Hidemi no Mikoto]], who met and piloted the boat in Oyamakuni, was later appointed as the founder of Oyamakuni. * In genealogical documents, Shiinetsuhiko is also called '''Uzubiko''', and in the "Yamato Shukune and Uminao" genealogy in the "Hyakke Genealogical Manuscripts" and the "Moro Genealogical Records", he is regarded as the grandson of Nurutama no Mikoto, the son of '''[[Watatsumi|Watatsumi Toyotamahiko-no-mikoto]]'''.
=== Clan ===
The name of the clan changed as the name of the country changed (Oyamatoji, Oyotokuji, Yamatoji, etc.). In [[Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]]10 ([[681|681]]), Yamato Ryūmarō was given the [[Muraji|Ren]] surname, in [[12]] the family became Ren, in [[14]] the surname became Imazun, and in [[Nara period|Nara era]] Yamato Nagaoka and Mizumori were promoted to [[Sukune|Sukune]]. Their distant ancestor, Shiinetsuhiko, is said to be the great-grandson of [[Watatsumi|Watatsumi Toyotamahiko-no-mikoto]], and the family is classified as a Jigion by deity. He is also said to be of the same clan as the Yagi clan and the [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]]. Descendants include the Kubiki Kunizo, Aomi, Akashi Kunizo, and Kibi-Kaibe clans.
Note that the Wa are descended from [[Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong]], king of [[Baekje|Baekje]], so they are a separate lineage.
== base == Is it Cape Anaso-eup Oyachooka (former shrine site of Yamato Shrine)?
=== ruling domain === The area ruled by the Kunizo was then called '''Yamato Province''', later ''''[[Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]''', which is now [[Nara Prefecture]] [[Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]] and the surrounding area. (eastern part of the Nara Basin) was also ruled.
== Ujigami ==
* ''''[[Ōyamato Shrine|Yamato Za Okunitama Shrine]]''' (Ōyamato Shrine) *: Or, Yamato Okunitama Shrine. In the 7th year of Emperor Sujin's reign ([[91 BC|91 BC]]), Princess Nu-no-shiroiru, who was the chief priestess of the shrine, lost her hair and became too thin to continue her rituals, and in a dream, she was told by the oracle [[Emperor]] that "If you make Iso-nagao City the host of a shrine to the deity Yamato Okunitama, the kingdom will be in peace. Suinin|Taruhito]] dynasty, Ichiso-Nagao City was founded as a shrine to the deity of the Great Goddess of the Sea of Japan.
=== Tomb ===
* ''''Nakayama Otsuka Tumulus''' *: A front-recessed circular mound constructed in the early Kofun period, with Mikoto Sukune assumed to be its burial deity. * '''Hatagozuka burial mound'''. *: A forward-backward burial mound constructed in the early Kofun period, with Ichiso-Nagao City as the supposed interred object. * '''Shimoikeyama Tumulus'''. *: A forward-backward mound constructed in the early Kofun period, with Gojuno Sukune as the supposed interred person.
== Person ==
* '''Mimonosukune'''. *: [[Kofun period|Kofun dynasty]] figure and national governor of the [[Emperor Sujin|Sungjin dynasty]]. Fifth grandson of Shiinetsuhiko. * ''''Ichishino-Nagaoichi'''' *: A Kofun period figure and national governor of the [[Emperor Suinin|Taruhin dynasty]]. Son of [[Mikazukune]]. * ''''Isono Sukune''''. *: A figure of the Kofun period, son of Ichiso-Nagaoichi. * '''Kate-no-Sukune'''. *: A figure of the Kofun period, son of Iguno Sukune. * '''Naruko Sukune'''. *: A personage of the Kofun period, son of Mukote Sukune. * '''Yamato Agoko Kagome'''. *: Kofun-era figure and national governor from the [[Emperor Nintoku|Nintoku dynasty]] to the [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku dynasty]]. Son of Naruko Shukune and brother of Somaro. He was almost killed by [[Emperor Richū|Renchū]] in a dispute over the succession to the throne, but he was saved by offering his sister Hinohime as an uname. Since then, it has become a custom for the Yamato clan to offer Une no Ehime to the Great King's family. * ''''Somaro'''' *: A figure of the Kofun period and brother of Agokago. Also known as Maro Shukune, he was given the surname Oyamachinao. * '''Nakuino-Atai'''. *: A figure of the Kofun period, son of Soumaro. * '''Yukiniwa no Atai''''. *: A personage of the Kofun period, son of Namaikinao. * '''Tehiko Atai'''. *: Kofun period general and [[Emperor Kinmei|Kinmei Dynasty]] kokuzo. Son of Yukiniwa Nao, he was active in the [[Silla|Silla]] wars against [[Silla]].
== descendants ==
* Oyamato Gohyakusoku *: There is a genealogical record that he was a son of Oyamato Ryumaro (Suzuki Shinnen, Hyakke Genealogical Manuscripts, vol. 21). Oyamakuninuzo, Ujigami, Gyobu Shosuke. *: Yamato Nagaoka *: [[Nara period|Nara era]] jurist. Son of Iotari (io-tari) of Oyamato. He was involved in the compilation of the [[Yōrō Code|Yōrō Ritsudō]]. His first name was Oyamato Imosun Kotohito. He later became Oyotoku Sukune, then Oyotoku Sukune, then Oyotoku Sukune again, and finally Yamato Sukune Nagaoka. He was also a Gyobu Shosuke, Minbu Daisuke and Kunmiya Daitada, Settsu Ryo, Mikawa Mamoru, Kawachi Mamoru, deputy chief of the Saikai Province patrol, and Sakyo Daio. Yamato-kunizo, Sho shikko-ranked. Compilation of the Reform Ritsuryo. * Yamato Tateko. *: A palm retainer of the Nimyo dynasty. He was given the surname of Asaomi. Later became a courtier and a fourth-ranked attendant.
== Family tree == This is the family tree of the origins of the Yamato Kuni no Miyatsuko<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kondō |first=Toshitaka |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/31126771 |title=Kodai gōzoku keizu shūran |last2=近藤敏喬. |date=Heisei 5 [1993] |publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan |others=敏喬. 近藤 |isbn=4-490-20225-3 |edition=Shohan |location=Tōkyō |oclc=31126771}}</ref>.{{familytree/start}} {{familytree|001|border=0|001=[[Amaterasu|{{Bgcolor|#F2CEE0|Amaterasu}}]]}} {{familytree||!||border=0}} {{familytree|001|border=0|001=[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]}} {{familytree||!||border=0}} {{familytree|001|11=003|002=[[Konohanasakuya-hime|{{Bgcolor|#F2CEE0|Konohanasakuya-hime}}]]|001=[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]|border=0|14=|13=|12=|10=|~|9=|8=|7=|6=|5=002|4=~|y|003=[[Watatsumi]]）}} {{familytree||13=v|21=|20=|19=.|18=-|17=-|16=+|15=-|14=-|12=-||11=,|10=|9=|8=|7=|6=|5=!|4=||border=0}} {{familytree||10=|003={{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命}}|002=[[Toyotama-hime|{{Bgcolor|#F2CEE0|Toyotama-hime}}]]|001=[[Hoori]]|border=0|12=004|11=003|9=!||8=002|7=~|6=y|5=~|4=001||004={{ill|Nurutama-no-mikoto|ja|布留多摩命}}}} {{familytree||13=!|21=|20=|19=!|18=|17=|16=!|15=|14=|12=||11=|10=|9=|8=!|7=|6=|5=|4=||border=0}} {{familytree||10=002|003=（[[Azumi people]]）|002='''{{Bgcolor|#F2CEE0|Tamayori-hime}}'''|001='''[[Ugayafukiaezu]]'''|border=0|12=004|11=003|9=~||8=y|7=001|6=|5=|4=||004=（{{Interlanguage link|Owari Kuni-no-miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造}}, {{Interlanguage link|Yamato Kuni-no-miyatsuko|ja|倭国造}})}} {{familytree||13=-|21=|20=|19=|18=|17=|16=|15=|14=.|12=-||11=v|10=^|9=-|8=v|7=-|6=-|5=,|4=||border=0}} {{familytree||10=|003={{ill|Mikeiri no Mikoto|ja|御毛沼命}}|002={{ill|Inahi|ja|稲飯命}}|001={{ill|Itsuse|ja|五瀬命}}|border=0|12=|11=|9=||8=|7=004|6=003|5=002|4=001||004=[[Jimmu]]}} {{familytree||13=|21=|20=|19=|18=|17=|16=|15=|14=!|12=||11=|10=|9=|8=|7=|6=|5=|4=||border=0}} {{familytree||12=|border=0|18=|17=|16=|15=|14=|13=001|11=||10=|9=|8=|7=|6=|5=|4=||001=（[[Imperial House of Japan]]）}} {{familytree/end}}

Their [[Ujigami]] is {{Ill|Yamato Okunitama|lt=Yamato Okunitama|ja|倭大国魂神}} of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]

== Overview ==
=== Names === In the Kiki they are called {{Nihongo|2=倭国造}} and in the [[Kujiki]] they are called {{Nihongo|2=大倭国造}}
=== ancestor ===
* In the Kojiki, after [[Emperor Jimmu|Wakamoge-no-Mikoto]] left Takashima Palace in [[Kibi Province|Kibi Province]], he went to Hayasukumon {{Efn|thought to be the Toyo Straits, Akashi Straits, Kibi, etc.}}. and appointed '''Konezu-no-hiko''', a Kunitsugami deity who had guided him and made offerings to him, to the position of Yamakazo. * In the Nihon-shoki (Chronicles of Japan), Shiinetsuhiko (Chinhiko), whom he met soon after leaving Hyuga, was appointed for the first time as the creator of Yamato in the 2nd year of Emperor Jinmu's reign, in recognition of his success in the Jinmu Eastern Expedition. * The "Nihon-shoki" (Chronicles of Japan) "Kokuzo honki" tells that Shiinetsuhiko-no-mikoto, a grandson of [[Hoori|Hiko Hidemi no Mikoto]], who met and piloted the boat in Oyamakuni, was later appointed as the founder of Oyamakuni. * In genealogical documents, Shiinetsuhiko is also called '''Uzubiko''', and in the "Yamato Shukune and Uminao" genealogy in the "Hyakke Genealogical Manuscripts" and the "Moro Genealogical Records", he is regarded as the grandson of Nurutama no Mikoto, the son of '''[[Watatsumi|Watatsumi Toyotamahiko-no-mikoto]]'''.
=== Clan ===
The name of the clan changed as the name of the country changed (Oyamatoji, Oyotokuji, Yamatoji, etc.). In [[Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]]10 ([[681|681]]), Yamato Ryūmarō was given the [[Muraji|Ren]] surname, in [[12]] the family became Ren, in [[14]] the surname became Imazun, and in [[Nara period|Nara era]] Yamato Nagaoka and Mizumori were promoted to [[Sukune|Sukune]]. Their distant ancestor, Shiinetsuhiko, is said to be the great-grandson of [[Watatsumi|Watatsumi Toyotamahiko-no-mikoto]], and the family is classified as a Jigion by deity. He is also said to be of the same clan as the Yagi clan and the [[Azumi people|Azumi clan]]. Descendants include the Kubiki Kunizo, Aomi, Akashi Kunizo, and Kibi-Kaibe clans.
Note that the Wa are descended from [[Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong]], king of [[Baekje|Baekje]], so they are a separate lineage.
== base == Is it Cape Anaso-eup Oyachooka (former shrine site of Yamato Shrine)?
=== ruling domain === The area ruled by the Kunizo was then called '''Yamato Province''', later ''''[[Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]''', which is now [[Nara Prefecture]] [[Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]] and the surrounding area. (eastern part of the Nara Basin) was also ruled.
== Ujigami ==
* ''''[[Ōyamato Shrine|Yamato Za Okunitama Shrine]]''' (Ōyamato Shrine) *: Or, Yamato Okunitama Shrine. In the 7th year of Emperor Sujin's reign ([[91 BC|91 BC]]), Princess Nu-no-shiroiru, who was the chief priestess of the shrine, lost her hair and became too thin to continue her rituals, and in a dream, she was told by the oracle [[Emperor]] that "If you make Iso-nagao City the host of a shrine to the deity Yamato Okunitama, the kingdom will be in peace. Suinin|Taruhito]] dynasty, Ichiso-Nagao City was founded as a shrine to the deity of the Great Goddess of the Sea of Japan.
=== Tomb ===
* ''''Nakayama Otsuka Tumulus''' *: A front-recessed circular mound constructed in the early Kofun period, with Mikoto Sukune assumed to be its burial deity. * '''Hatagozuka burial mound'''. *: A forward-backward burial mound constructed in the early Kofun period, with Ichiso-Nagao City as the supposed interred object. * '''Shimoikeyama Tumulus'''. *: A forward-backward mound constructed in the early Kofun period, with Gojuno Sukune as the supposed interred person.
== Person ==
* '''Mimonosukune'''. *: [[Kofun period|Kofun dynasty]] figure and national governor of the [[Emperor Sujin|Sungjin dynasty]]. Fifth grandson of Shiinetsuhiko. * ''''Ichishino-Nagaoichi'''' *: A Kofun period figure and national governor of the [[Emperor Suinin|Taruhin dynasty]]. Son of [[Mikazukune]]. * ''''Isono Sukune''''. *: A figure of the Kofun period, son of Ichiso-Nagaoichi. * '''Kate-no-Sukune'''. *: A figure of the Kofun period, son of Iguno Sukune. * '''Naruko Sukune'''. *: A personage of the Kofun period, son of Mukote Sukune. * '''Yamato Agoko Kagome'''. *: Kofun-era figure and national governor from the [[Emperor Nintoku|Nintoku dynasty]] to the [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku dynasty]]. Son of Naruko Shukune and brother of Somaro. He was almost killed by [[Emperor Richū|Renchū]] in a dispute over the succession to the throne, but he was saved by offering his sister Hinohime as an uname. Since then, it has become a custom for the Yamato clan to offer Une no Ehime to the Great King's family. * ''''Somaro'''' *: A figure of the Kofun period and brother of Agokago. Also known as Maro Shukune, he was given the surname Oyamachinao. * '''Nakuino-Atai'''. *: A figure of the Kofun period, son of Soumaro. * '''Yukiniwa no Atai''''. *: A personage of the Kofun period, son of Namaikinao. * '''Tehiko Atai'''. *: Kofun period general and [[Emperor Kinmei|Kinmei Dynasty]] kokuzo. Son of Yukiniwa Nao, he was active in the [[Silla|Silla]] wars against [[Silla]].
== descendants ==
* Oyamato Gohyakusoku *: There is a genealogical record that he was a son of Oyamato Ryumaro (Suzuki Shinnen, Hyakke Genealogical Manuscripts, vol. 21). Oyamakuninuzo, Ujigami, Gyobu Shosuke. *: Yamato Nagaoka *: [[Nara period|Nara era]] jurist. Son of Iotari (io-tari) of Oyamato. He was involved in the compilation of the [[Yōrō Code|Yōrō Ritsudō]]. His first name was Oyamato Imosun Kotohito. He later became Oyotoku Sukune, then Oyotoku Sukune, then Oyotoku Sukune again, and finally Yamato Sukune Nagaoka. He was also a Gyobu Shosuke, Minbu Daisuke and Kunmiya Daitada, Settsu Ryo, Mikawa Mamoru, Kawachi Mamoru, deputy chief of the Saikai Province patrol, and Sakyo Daio. Yamato-kunizo, Sho shikko-ranked. Compilation of the Reform Ritsuryo. * Yamato Tateko. *: A palm retainer of the Nimyo dynasty. He was given the surname of Asaomi. Later became a courtier and a fourth-ranked attendant.
== Family tree == This is the family tree of the origins of the Yamato Kuni no Miyatsuko<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kondō |first=Toshitaka |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/31126771 |title=Kodai gōzoku keizu shūran |last2=近藤敏喬. |date=Heisei 5 [1993] |publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan |others=敏喬. 近藤 |isbn=4-490-20225-3 |edition=Shohan |location=Tōkyō |oclc=31126771}}</ref>.{{familytree/start}} {{familytree|001|border=0|001=[[Amaterasu|{{Bgcolor|#F2CEE0|Amaterasu}}]]}} {{familytree||!||border=0}} {{familytree|001|border=0|001=[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]}} {{familytree||!||border=0}} {{familytree|001|11=003|002=[[Konohanasakuya-hime|{{Bgcolor|#F2CEE0|Konohanasakuya-hime}}]]|001=[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]|border=0|14=|13=|12=|10=|~|9=|8=|7=|6=|5=002|4=~|y|003=[[Watatsumi]]）}} {{familytree||13=v|21=|20=|19=.|18=-|17=-|16=+|15=-|14=-|12=-||11=,|10=|9=|8=|7=|6=|5=!|4=||border=0}} {{familytree||10=|003={{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命}}|002=[[Toyotama-hime|{{Bgcolor|#F2CEE0|Toyotama-hime}}]]|001=[[Hoori]]|border=0|12=004|11=003|9=!||8=002|7=~|6=y|5=~|4=001||004={{ill|Nurutama-no-mikoto|ja|布留多摩命}}}} {{familytree||13=!|21=|20=|19=!|18=|17=|16=!|15=|14=|12=||11=|10=|9=|8=!|7=|6=|5=|4=||border=0}} {{familytree||10=002|003=（[[Azumi people]]）|002='''{{Bgcolor|#F2CEE0|Tamayori-hime}}'''|001='''[[Ugayafukiaezu]]'''|border=0|12=004|11=003|9=~||8=y|7=001|6=|5=|4=||004=（{{Interlanguage link|Owari Kuni-no-miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造}}, {{Interlanguage link|Yamato Kuni-no-miyatsuko|ja|倭国造}})}} {{familytree||13=-|21=|20=|19=|18=|17=|16=|15=|14=.|12=-||11=v|10=^|9=-|8=v|7=-|6=-|5=,|4=||border=0}} {{familytree||10=|003={{ill|Mikeiri|lt=Mikeiri no Mikoto|ja|御毛沼命}}|002={{ill|Inahi|ja|稲飯命}}|001={{ill|Itsuse|ja|五瀬命}}|border=0|12=|11=|9=||8=|7=004|6=003|5=002|4=001||004=[[Jimmu]]}} {{familytree||13=|21=|20=|19=|18=|17=|16=|15=|14=!|12=||11=|10=|9=|8=|7=|6=|5=|4=||border=0}} {{familytree||12=|border=0|18=|17=|16=|15=|14=|13=001|11=||10=|9=|8=|7=|6=|5=|4=||001=（[[Imperial House of Japan]]）}} {{familytree/end}}
== Notes == <references group="注釈" /> <references /> {{notelist}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese noble family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[Yamato Province]]|home province=[[Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja01s3200.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}}} '''Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' was a title held by the clan who ruled the central region of the later [[Yamato Province]].{{Cite journal |last=Yōko |first=ISSE |date=2019 |title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: "Misunderstandings" and the Historical Facts of the Kiki |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868 |journal=Japan Review |issue=34 |pages=139–160 |jstor=26864868 |issn=0915-0986}} Kuni no Miyatsuko were regional rulers subordinate to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. After the position was abolished they remained prominent as the priests of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. Other kuni no miyatsuko this happened to include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],https://archive.ph/wip/08goA and the {{ill|Amabe clan|lt=Amabe clan|ja|海部氏}} of [[Kono Shrine]]{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web |title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records" |url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global |language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042}}{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2012-11-10 |title=Teeuwen on Shinto |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}} https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation |title=Book V |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-05-04}} == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii-2.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[Hibara Shrine]], at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web |title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26 |url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Japanese Experts Net |language=en-US}} And Shinetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/id=9159|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the {{Ill|Yaoyorozu no Kami|lt=eighty myriad deities|simple|Yaoyorozu no Kami}}. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} [[simple:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web |title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It |url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Sengoku Daimyo |date=April 2022 |language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]] == Notes == {{notelist}} ===References=== {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book |last=Aston |first=William George |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London) |year=1896 |isbn=9780524053478 |volume=1 |place=London |oclc=448337491 |author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web |author=JHTI |year=2002 |title=Nihon Shoki |url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html |access-date=2019-08-23 |work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI) |publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book |last=Ujiya |first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟) |title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀) |publisher=Kodansha |year=1988 |isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5 |volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book |last=Chamberlain |first=Basil Hall |title=The Kojiki |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1919 |oclc=1882339 |author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book |last=Takeda |first=Yukichi (武田祐吉) |title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記) |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1977 |isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Draft Categories| [[Category:Japanese history stubs]] [[Category:History of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Province]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:Yamato clan]] }} [[ja:倭国造]]

{{Short description|Japanese noble family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[Yamato Province]]|home province=[[Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja01s3200.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}}} '''Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' was a title held by the clan who ruled the central region of the later [[Yamato Province]].{{Cite journal |last=Yōko |first=ISSE |date=2019 |title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: "Misunderstandings" and the Historical Facts of the Kiki |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868 |journal=Japan Review |issue=34 |pages=139–160 |jstor=26864868 |issn=0915-0986}} Kuni no Miyatsuko were regional rulers subordinate to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. After the position was abolished they remained prominent as the priests of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. Other kuni no miyatsuko this happened to include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],https://archive.ph/wip/08goA and the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web |title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records" |url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global |language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042}}{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2012-11-10 |title=Teeuwen on Shinto |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}} https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation |title=Book V |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-05-04}} == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii-2.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[Hibara Shrine]], at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web |title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26 |url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Japanese Experts Net |language=en-US}} And Shinetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/id=9159|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the {{Ill|Yaoyorozu no Kami|lt=eighty myriad deities|simple|Yaoyorozu no Kami}}. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} [[simple:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web |title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It |url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Sengoku Daimyo |date=April 2022 |language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]] == Notes == {{notelist}} ===References=== {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book |last=Aston |first=William George |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London) |year=1896 |isbn=9780524053478 |volume=1 |place=London |oclc=448337491 |author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web |author=JHTI |year=2002 |title=Nihon Shoki |url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html |access-date=2019-08-23 |work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI) |publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book |last=Ujiya |first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟) |title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀) |publisher=Kodansha |year=1988 |isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5 |volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book |last=Chamberlain |first=Basil Hall |title=The Kojiki |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1919 |oclc=1882339 |author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book |last=Takeda |first=Yukichi (武田祐吉) |title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記) |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1977 |isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Draft Categories| [[Category:Japanese history stubs]] [[Category:History of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Province]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:Yamato clan]] }} [[ja:倭国造]]

{{Short description|Japanese noble family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[Yamato Province]]|home province=[[Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja01s3200.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}}} '''Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' was a title held by the clan who ruled the central region of the later [[Yamato Province]].{{Cite journal |last=Yōko |first=ISSE |date=2019 |title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: "Misunderstandings" and the Historical Facts of the Kiki |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868 |journal=Japan Review |issue=34 |pages=139–160 |jstor=26864868 |issn=0915-0986}} Kuni no Miyatsuko were regional rulers subordinate to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. After the position was abolished they remained prominent as the priests of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. Other kuni no miyatsuko this happened to include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],https://archive.ph/wip/08goA and the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web |title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records" |url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global |language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042}}{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2012-11-10 |title=Teeuwen on Shinto |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}} https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation |title=Book V |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-05-04}} == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii-2.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[Hibara Shrine]], at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web |title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26 |url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Japanese Experts Net |language=en-US}} And Shinetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/id=9159|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the {{Ill|Yaoyorozu no Kami|lt=eighty myriad deities|simple|Yaoyorozu no Kami}}. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} [[simple:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web |title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It |url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Sengoku Daimyo |date=April 2022 |language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]] == Notes == {{notelist}} ===References=== {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book |last=Aston |first=William George |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London) |year=1896 |isbn=9780524053478 |volume=1 |place=London |oclc=448337491 |author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web |author=JHTI |year=2002 |title=Nihon Shoki |url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html |access-date=2019-08-23 |work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI) |publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book |last=Ujiya |first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟) |title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀) |publisher=Kodansha |year=1988 |isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5 |volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book |last=Chamberlain |first=Basil Hall |title=The Kojiki |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1919 |oclc=1882339 |author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book |last=Takeda |first=Yukichi (武田祐吉) |title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記) |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1977 |isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] [[Category:Japanese history stubs]] [[Category:History of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Province]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:Yamato clan]] [[ja:倭国造]]

By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the {{Ill|Yaoyorozu no Kami|lt=eighty myriad deities|simple|Yaoyorozu no Kami}}. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} [[simple:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web |title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It |url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Sengoku Daimyo |date=April 2022 |language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]] == Notes == {{notelist}} ===References=== {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book |last=Aston |first=William George |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London) |year=1896 |isbn=9780524053478 |volume=1 |place=London |oclc=448337491 |author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web |author=JHTI |year=2002 |title=Nihon Shoki |url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html |access-date=2019-08-23 |work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI) |publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book |last=Ujiya |first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟) |title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀) |publisher=Kodansha |year=1988 |isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5 |volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book |last=Chamberlain |first=Basil Hall |title=The Kojiki |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1919 |oclc=1882339 |author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book |last=Takeda |first=Yukichi (武田祐吉) |title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記) |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1977 |isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] [[Category:Japanese history stubs]] [[Category:History of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Province]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:Yamato clan]] [[ja:倭国造]]

{{Short description|Japanese noble family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[Yamato Province]]|home province=[[Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja01s3200.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}}} '''Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' was a title held by the clan who ruled the central region of the later [[Yamato Province]].{{Cite journal |last=Yōko |first=ISSE |date=2019 |title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: "Misunderstandings" and the Historical Facts of the Kiki |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868 |journal=Japan Review |issue=34 |pages=139–160 |jstor=26864868 |issn=0915-0986}} Kuni no Miyatsuko were regional rulers subordinate to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. After the position was abolished they remained prominent as the priests of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. Other kuni no miyatsuko this happened to include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],https://archive.ph/wip/08goA and the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web |title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records" |url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global |language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2012-11-10 |title=Teeuwen on Shinto |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}} https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation |title=Book V |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-05-04}} == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii-2.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[Hibara Shrine]], at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web |title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26 |url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Japanese Experts Net |language=en-US}} And Shinetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/id=9159|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the {{Ill|Yaoyorozu no Kami|lt=eighty myriad deities|simple|Yaoyorozu no Kami}}. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} [[simple:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web |title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It |url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Sengoku Daimyo |date=April 2022 |language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]] == Notes == {{notelist}} ===References=== {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book |last=Aston |first=William George |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London) |year=1896 |isbn=9780524053478 |volume=1 |place=London |oclc=448337491 |author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web |author=JHTI |year=2002 |title=Nihon Shoki |url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html |access-date=2019-08-23 |work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI) |publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book |last=Ujiya |first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟) |title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀) |publisher=Kodansha |year=1988 |isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5 |volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book |last=Chamberlain |first=Basil Hall |title=The Kojiki |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1919 |oclc=1882339 |author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book |last=Takeda |first=Yukichi (武田祐吉) |title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記) |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1977 |isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] [[Category:Japanese history stubs]] [[Category:History of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Province]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:Yamato clan]] [[ja:倭国造]] {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}}

(Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book |last=Ujiya |first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟) |title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀) |publisher=Kodansha |year=1988 |isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5 |volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book |last=Chamberlain |first=Basil Hall |title=The Kojiki |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1919 |oclc=1882339 |author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book |last=Takeda |first=Yukichi (武田祐吉) |title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記) |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1977 |isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] [[Category:Japanese history stubs]] [[Category:History of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Province]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:Yamato clan]] [[ja:倭国造]] {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}}
