|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2012-11-10 |title=Teeuwen on Shinto |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation |title=Book V |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-05-04}} == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja, torii closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]], at the foot of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} was given the position of governor of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] by [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web |title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26 |url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Japanese Experts Net |language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web |date=2023-10-26 |title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-12-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |archive-date=2023-10-26 }} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Ōyamato Shrine|Oyamato Shrine]], near [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] named {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[:en:Divination|Divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|qq=}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web |title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It |url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Sengoku Daimyo |date=April 2022 |language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Okunitama Shrine|qq=}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[:en:Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]] == Notes == {{notelist}} ===Overview== ==Overview== ==References=== {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book |last=Aston |first=William George |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London) |year=1896 |isbn=9780524053478 |volume=1 |place=London |oclc=448337491 |author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web |author=JHTI |year=2002 |title=Nihon Shoki |url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html |access-date=2019-08-23 |work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI) |publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book |last=Ujiya |first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟) |title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀) |publisher=Kodansha |year=1988 |isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5 |volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book |last=Chamberlain |first=Basil Hall |title=The Kojiki |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1919 |oclc=1882339 |author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book |last=Takeda |first=Yukichi (武田祐吉) |title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記) |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1977 |isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[en:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Clan Yamato]] [[ja:倭国造]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[simple:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] {{wikidata link|Q11387078}}

[[en:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[fr:Clan Yamato]][[ja:倭国造]][[simple:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11387078]] Wikidata name: Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese noble family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|home province=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}}} '''Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' was a title held by the clan{{Efn|Yamato no Atai clan}} who ruled the central region of the later [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]].{{Cite journal |last=Yōko |first=ISSE |date=2019 |title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: "Misunderstandings" and the Historical Facts of the Kiki |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868 |journal=Japan Review |issue=34 |pages=139–160 |jstor=26864868 |issn=0915-0986}} Kuni no Miyatsuko were regional rulers subordinate to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. After the position was abolished they remained prominent as the priests of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. Other kuni no miyatsuko this happened to include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 and the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web |title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records" |url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global |language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2012-11-10 |title=Teeuwen on Shinto |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation |title=Book V |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-05-04}} == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja, torii closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]], at the foot of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} was given the position of governor of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] by [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor their ancestor.{{Cite web |date=2023-10-26 |title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-12-04 There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Ōyamato Shrine|Oyamato Shrine]], near [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] named {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}}} '''Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' was a title held by the clan{{Efn|Yamato no Atai clan}} who ruled the central region of the later [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]].{{Cite journal |last=Yōko |first=ISSE |date=2019 |title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: "Misunderstandings" and the Historical Facts of the Kiki |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868 |journal=Japan Review |issue=34 |pages=139–160 |jstor=26864868 |issn=0915-0986}} Kuni no Miyatsuko were regional rulers subordinate to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. After the position was abolished they remained prominent as the priests of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. Other kuni no miyatsuko this happened to include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 and the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web |title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records" |url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global |language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2012-11-10 |title=Teeuwen on Shinto |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation |title=Book V |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-05-04}} == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja, torii closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]], at the foot of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} was given the position of governor of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] by [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web |title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26 |url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Japanese Experts Net |language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web |date=2023-10-26 |title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-12-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |archive-date=2023-10-26 }} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Ōyamato Shrine|Oyamato Shrine]], near [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] named {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[:en:Divination|Divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|qq=}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web |title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It |url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Sengoku Daimyo |date=April 2022 |language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Okunitama Shrine|qq=}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[:en:Princess [[en:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[fr:Clan Yamato]][[ja:倭国造]][[simple:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11387078]] Wikidata name: Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese noble family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|home province=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}}} '''Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' was a title held by the clan{{Efn|Yamato no Atai clan}} who ruled the central region of the later [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]].{{Cite journal |last=Yōko |first=ISSE |date=2019 |title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: "Misunderstandings" and the Historical Facts of the Kiki |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868 |journal=Japan Review |issue=34 |pages=139–160 |jstor=26864868 |issn=0915-0986}} Kuni no Miyatsuko were regional rulers subordinate to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. After the position was abolished they remained prominent as the priests of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. Other kuni no miyatsuko this happened to include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 and the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web |title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records" |url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global |language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2012-11-10 |title=Teeuwen on Shinto |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation |title=Book V |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-05-04}} == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja, torii closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]], at the foot of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} was given the position of governor of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] by [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web |title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26 |url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Japanese Experts Net |language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web |date=2023-10-26 |title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-12-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |archive-date=2023-10-26 }} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Ōyamato Shrine|Oyamato Shrine]], near [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] named {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[:en:Divination|Divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|qq=}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web |title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It |url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Sengoku Daimyo |date=April 2022 |language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Okunitama Shrine|qq=}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[:en:Princess [[en:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[fr:Clan Yamato]][[ja:倭国造]][[simple:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11387078]] Wikidata name: Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese noble family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|home province=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}}} '''Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' was a title held by the clan{{Efn|Yamato no Atai clan}} who ruled the central region of the later [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]].{{Cite journal |last=Yōko |first=ISSE |date=2019 |title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: "Misunderstandings" and the Historical Facts of the Kiki |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868 |journal=Japan Review |issue=34 |pages=139–160 |jstor=26864868 |issn=0915-0986}} Kuni no Miyatsuko were regional rulers subordinate to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. After the position was abolished they remained prominent as the priests of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. Other kuni no miyatsuko this happened to include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 and the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web |title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records" |url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global |language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2012-11-10 |title=Teeuwen on Shinto |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation |title=Book V |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-05-04}} == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja, torii closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]], at the foot of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} was given the position of governor of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] by [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web |title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26 |url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Japanese Experts Net |language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web |date=2023-10-26 |title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-12-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |archive-date=2023-10-26 }} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Ōyamato Shrine|Oyamato Shrine]], near [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] named {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[:en:Divination|Divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|qq=}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web |title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It |url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Sengoku Daimyo |date=April 2022 |language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Okunitama Shrine|qq=}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[:en:Princess Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] named {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[:en:Divination|Divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|qq=}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web |title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It |url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Sengoku Daimyo |date=April 2022 |language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Okunitama Shrine|qq=}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[:en:Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]] == Notes == {{notelist}} ===Overview== ==Overview== ==References=== {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book |last=Aston |first=William George |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London) |year=1896 |isbn=9780524053478 |volume=1 |place=London |oclc=448337491 |author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web |author=JHTI |year=2002 |title=Nihon Shoki |url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html |access-date=2019-08-23 |work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI) |publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book |last=Ujiya |first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟) |title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀) |publisher=Kodansha |year=1988 |isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5 |volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book |last=Chamberlain |first=Basil Hall |title=The Kojiki |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1919 |oclc=1882339 |author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book |last=Takeda |first=Yukichi (武田祐吉) |title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記) |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1977 |isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[en:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Clan Yamato]] [[ja:倭国造]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[simple:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] {{wikidata link|Q11387078}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] {{wikidata link|Q11387078}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] to [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[en:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[fr:Clan Yamato]][[ja:倭国造]][[simple:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11387078]] Wikidata name: Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese noble family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|home province=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}}} '''Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' was a title held by the clan{{Efn|Yamato no Atai clan}} who ruled the central region of the later [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]].{{Cite journal |last=Yōko |first=ISSE |date=2019 |title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: "Misunderstandings" and the Historical Facts of the Kiki |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868 |journal=Japan Review |issue=34 |pages=139–160 |jstor=26864868 |issn=0915-0986}} Kuni no Miyatsuko were regional rulers subordinate to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. After the position was abolished they remained prominent as the priests of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. Other kuni no miyatsuko this happened to include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 and the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web |title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records" |url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global |language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2012-11-10 |title=Teeuwen on Shinto |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation |title=Book V |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-05-04}} == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja, torii closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]], at the foot of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} was given the position of governor of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] by [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web |title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26 |url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Japanese Experts Net |language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web |date=2023-10-26 |title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-12-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |archive-date=2023-10-26 }} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Ōyamato Shrine|Oyamato Shrine]], near [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] named {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[:en:Divination|Divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|qq=}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web |title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It |url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Sengoku Daimyo |date=April 2022 |language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Okunitama Shrine|qq=}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[:en:Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]] == Notes == {{notelist}} ===Overview== ==Overview== ==References=== {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book |last=Aston |first=William George |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London) |year=1896 |isbn=9780524053478 |volume=1 |place=London |oclc=448337491 |author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web |author=JHTI |year=2002 |title=Nihon Shoki |url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html |access-date=2019-08-23 |work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI) |publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book |last=Ujiya |first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟) |title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀) |publisher=Kodansha |year=1988 |isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5 |volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book |last=Chamberlain |first=Basil Hall |title=The Kojiki |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1919 |oclc=1882339 |author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book |last=Takeda |first=Yukichi (武田祐吉) |title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記) |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1977 |isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[en:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Clan Yamato]] [[ja:倭国造]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[simple:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] {{wikidata link|Q11387078}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] {{wikidata link|Q11387078}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]]

|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation |title=Book V |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-05-04}} == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja, torii closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]], at the foot of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} was given the position of governor of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] by [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web |title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26 |url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Japanese Experts Net |language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web |date=2023-10-26 |title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-12-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |archive-date=2023-10-26 }} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Ōyamato Shrine|Oyamato Shrine]], near [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] named {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[:en:Divination|Divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|qq=}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web |title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It |url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Sengoku Daimyo |date=April 2022 |language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Okunitama Shrine|qq=}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[:en:Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]] == Notes == {{notelist}} ===Overview== ==Overview== ==References=== {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book |last=Aston |first=William George |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299 |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London) |year=1896 |isbn=9780524053478 |volume=1 |place=London |oclc=448337491 |author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web |author=JHTI |year=2002 |title=Nihon Shoki |url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html |access-date=2019-08-23 |work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI) |publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book |last=Ujiya |first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟) |title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀) |publisher=Kodansha |year=1988 |isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5 |volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book |last=Chamberlain |first=Basil Hall |title=The Kojiki |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1919 |oclc=1882339 |author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book |last=Takeda |first=Yukichi (武田祐吉) |title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記) |publisher=Kadokawa |year=1977 |isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[en:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Clan Yamato]] [[ja:倭国造]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[simple:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] {{wikidata link|Q11387078}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] {{wikidata link|Q11387078}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

[[en:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[fr:Clan Yamato]][[ja:倭国造]][[simple:Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11387078]] Wikidata name: Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese noble family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|home province=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}}} '''Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' was a title held by the clan{{Efn|Yamato no Atai clan}} who ruled the central region of the later [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]].{{Cite journal |last=Yōko |first=ISSE |date=2019 |title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: "Misunderstandings" and the Historical Facts of the Kiki |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868 |journal=Japan Review |issue=34 |pages=139–160 |jstor=26864868 |issn=0915-0986}} Kuni no Miyatsuko were regional rulers subordinate to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. After the position was abolished they remained prominent as the priests of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. Other kuni no miyatsuko this happened to include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 and the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web |title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records" |url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global |language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2012-11-10 |title=Teeuwen on Shinto |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation |title=Book V |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-05-04}} == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja, torii closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]], at the foot of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} was given the position of governor of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] by [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web |title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26 |url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Japanese Experts Net |language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web |date=2023-10-26 |title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-12-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |archive-date=2023-10-26 }} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Ōyamato Shrine|Oyamato Shrine]], near [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] named {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[:en:Divination|Divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|qq=}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web |title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It |url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Sengoku Daimyo |date=April 2022 |language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Okunitama Shrine|qq=}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[:en:Princess {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}}} '''Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' was a title held by the clan{{Efn|Yamato no Atai clan}} who ruled the central region of the later [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]].{{Cite journal |last=Yōko |first=ISSE |date=2019 |title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: "Misunderstandings" and the Historical Facts of the Kiki |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868 |journal=Japan Review |issue=34 |pages=139–160 |jstor=26864868 |issn=0915-0986}} Kuni no Miyatsuko were regional rulers subordinate to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. After the position was abolished they remained prominent as the priests of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. Other kuni no miyatsuko this happened to include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 and the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web |title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records" |url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global |language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2012-11-10 |title=Teeuwen on Shinto |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation |title=Book V |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V |work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1 |access-date=2023-05-04}} == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja, torii closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]], at the foot of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} was given the position of governor of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] by [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web |title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26 |url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/ |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Japanese Experts Net |language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web |date=2023-10-26 |title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |access-date=2023-12-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159 |archive-date=2023-10-26 }} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Ōyamato Shrine|Oyamato Shrine]], near [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] named {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[:en:Divination|Divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|qq=}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web |title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It |url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61 |access-date=2023-05-04 |website=Sengoku Daimyo |date=April 2022 |language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Okunitama Shrine|qq=}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[:en:Princess
